title: Circles, Arc Length, and Sector Area
text:
1 Overview
Every circle has two basic measurements you already know: the area, which is \(\pi r^2\), and the circumference (the distance around the circle), which is \(2\pi r\) (or equivalently \(\pi d\), using the diameter). The radius \(r\) is the distance from the center to the edge, and the diameter \(d\) is twice the radius. These two formulas are connected! If you know one, you can always find the radius, and from the radius you can find the other.
A sector is a “slice of pie” cut from a circle — it’s the region between two radii and the arc that connects them. Every sector is defined by its central angle, the angle at the very center of the circle between the two radii. Since a full circle is \(360°\), a sector’s angle out of \(360°\) tells you exactly what fraction of the whole circle that sector represents. That means: sector area = \(\frac{\text{angle}}{360} \times \pi r^2\), and the curved edge of the sector, called the arc length, is found the same way: \(\frac{\text{angle}}{360} \times 2\pi r\). Whenever you see a sector problem, the first question to ask is: “What fraction of the whole circle am I dealing with?”



alt text








ImportantCommon Mistake



It’s easy to plug numbers straight into the area or circumference formula and forget to multiply by the angle fraction first. Before you calculate anything, always ask: “What fraction of the full circle am I working with?” Find that fraction first, then apply it to the whole circle’s area or circumference.








NoteConcept Check



A sector has a central angle of \(90°\). Before plugging into any formula, what fraction of the whole circle does this sector represent?






TipShow Answer





\(\dfrac{90}{360} = \dfrac{1}{4}\) — a \(90°\) sector is exactly one-quarter of the circle.





(Diagram note: for sector problems below, picture a circle with a pie-slice-shaped region shaded in, with the central angle labeled at the point where the two straight edges meet in the middle of the circle.)



2 Practice Questions

A circle has a radius of \(6\) cm. Find its area, in terms of \(\pi\).
A circle has a diameter of \(14\) in. Find its circumference, in terms of \(\pi\).
A sector has a central angle of \(60°\) and sits inside a circle with radius \(12\). Find the area of the sector, in terms of \(\pi\).
A sector has a central angle of \(90°\) and sits inside a circle with radius \(8\). Find the arc length of the sector, in terms of \(\pi\).
The shaded sector below has a central angle of \(45°\) and an area of \(8\pi\). Find the circumference of the whole circle.
A circle has a circumference of \(20\pi\). What is the area of a sector of that circle with a central angle of \(72°\)?
A circle has a circumference of \(36\) cm. What is the arc length of a sector with a central angle of \(50°\)?
A circle has a radius of \(6\). A sector of that circle has an area of \(6\pi\). What is the measure of the sector’s central angle?
A circle has a radius of \(4\). An arc of the circle has a length of \(2\pi\). What is the central angle of that arc, in degrees?
A circular pizza has a radius of \(7\) inches. One slice is cut with a central angle of \(45°\). What is the area of that slice, in terms of \(\pi\)?
A circular running track has a radius of \(50\) meters. If a runner jogs along an arc with a central angle of \(108°\), how far did the runner run? Give your answer in terms of \(\pi\).
An arc with a central angle of \(90°\) has a length of \(5\pi\). What is the area of the whole circle, in terms of \(\pi\)?

title: Practice Test 1
text:
NoteDirections



Work through the problems in any order, they are not arranged by difficulty. Show all work for partial credit, and simplify every answer unless told otherwise. Problems 1–17 are worth \(126\) total points; Problems 18–19 are bonus problems worth \(3\) points each.




1 Problems
1. [5 points] The right triangle below has a hypotenuse of \(25\) and legs of length \(7\) and \(\sqrt{N}\). Find \(N\).  —
2. [5 points] A bag contains \(3\) red balls and \(7\) blue balls. If Sam draws two balls from the bag, one at a time, without putting the first one back, what is the probability that both balls are blue?

3. [5 points] Jeremy is writing a sequence of integers. He writes \(2\) as his first number. Then he squares it and adds \(2\), writing down \(6\) next. He squares \(6\) and adds \(2\), writing down \(38\) as his third number. If Jeremy continues this square-and-add pattern, what will be the units digit of the \(22\)nd number in his list?

4. [10 points] The area of isosceles trapezoid \(ABCD\) is \(270\). Furthermore, \(AB = 10\), \(DC = 26\), and \(AD = BC\) (the legs are congruent). If \(\angle D = \angle C\), what is the length of leg \(AD\)?



alt text



5. [5 points] In \(\triangle ABC\), we have \(AB = AC = 9\) and \(\angle B = 58°\). Find the measure of \(\angle A\). 

6. [10 points] Lizzie rolls two dice. What is the probability that the sum of the dice is:
(a) divisible by \(4\)? (b) at least \(8\)? (c) divisible by \(4\) and at least \(8\)?

7. [5 points] Grogg surveyed \(150\) beasts at his school about their favorite drink. The pie chart below summarizes his results. If \(45\) of the beasts preferred juice, what is the value of \(x\)?



alt text



8. [10 points] Quadrilateral \(ABCD\) has right angles at \(A\) and \(D\). Side \(AD = 5\). If \(\angle B = 45°\), find the length of side \(BC\).



alt text



9. [5 points] How many \(3\)-digit multiples of \(17\) are there?

10. [10 points] Jamie makes \(75\%\) of her serves in volleyball. What is the probability that she misses her next two serves in a row?

11. [5 points] Jenna is training for a swim meet. The bar chart below shows the number of laps she swam each day this week.



alt text


(a) Find the mean number of laps swum each day. (b) Find the median number of laps swum each day. (c) Find the mode of the number of laps swum each day. (d) Find the range of the number of laps swum each day.

12. [10 points] A polygon is made by shading in unit squares on a grid.



alt text


(a) What is the smallest possible perimeter of such a polygon with area \(18\)? (b) What is the largest possible perimeter of such a polygon with area \(18\)?

13. [5 points] Find the area of the shaded triangle below.
![[Pasted image 20260720203818.png]]

14. [10 points] There are \(180\) creatures at Wizard Academy. The number of creatures with magic wands is three times the number of creatures with spellbooks. A quarter of the creatures with spellbooks also have wands. If \(30\) creatures have neither wands nor spellbooks, how many creatures have wands?



alt text



15. [5 points] Find the area of the irregularly-shaped hexagon below.



alt text



16. [10 points] The shaded sector below has a central angle of \(50°\) and an area of \(5\pi\). Find the circumference of the circle.



alt text



17. [5 points] A set of seven single-digit whole numbers has a unique mode of \(4\). What is the largest possible value of the median of these numbers?

18. [Bonus 3 points] A certain polygon has four angles that measure \(130°\), and the rest of its angles measure \(160°\). How many sides does the polygon have?

19. [Bonus 3 points] In right triangle \(PQR\), \(\angle Q = 90°\), \(PQ = 9\), and \(QR = 12\). Segment \(QS\) is drawn from \(Q\) perpendicular to the hypotenuse \(PR\), meeting it at point \(S\). Find the length of \(QS\).



alt text

title: Optimization:
text:
Optimization problems ask you to find the largest or smallest possible value that still follows a set of rules. You could try to guess-and-check every possibility, but there’s a faster and more reliable way to think about it: direction.
For every number in the problem that you’re allowed to choose, ask yourself:

“If I make this number bigger, does the thing I’m trying to maximize (or minimize) get bigger, get smaller, or stay the same?”

Once you know the direction, you don’t need to guess anymore, you push that number as far as the rules allow, straight toward the extreme case the direction points to.






TipThe Big Idea




Bigger helps → push that number as high as the rules allow.
Bigger hurts → push that number as low as the rules allow.





Take a problem like: a set of six single-digit whole numbers has a unique mode, and you want the largest possible median. You have a required number that must repeat (the mode), and several other numbers you get to choose freely, as long as none of them accidentally repeats often enough to also become a mode.
Ask the direction question: for six sorted numbers, the median depends on the 3rd and 4th values. If you make one of your free numbers bigger, does the median get bigger? Yes raising a free number (as long as it’s still larger than the required repeated number) pushes the sorted list’s upper half higher. Direction: bigger helps. So you push every free number as high as it’s allowed to go, and just as importantly you place the required repeated number as low as possible, out of the median’s way.






NoteConcept Check



Two numbers add up to \(20\). Compare \(9 \times 11\) to \(2 \times 18\). As two numbers with a fixed sum get closer together, does their product get bigger or smaller?






TipShow Answer





Bigger. \(9 \times 11 = 99\), but \(2 \times 18 = 36\). When two numbers have a fixed sum, their product is largest when the numbers are as close together as possible, and smallest when they’re as far apart as possible. Keep this pattern in mind, it shows up again below with perimeter!








Now picture a rectangle with a fixed area. If you increase one side length, the other side length has to shrink to keep the same area. What happens to the perimeter (the sum of all four sides) as you do this?
Direction: as the two side lengths move apart from each other, the perimeter grows. As they move closer together — toward being equal, i.e., a square — the perimeter shrinks. This is the exact same pattern from the Concept Check above, just applied to a sum (perimeter) instead of a product.

Minimum perimeter → make the shape as close to a square as possible.
Maximum perimeter → stretch it out as long and thin as possible (or, for blocky grid shapes, make it as zig-zaggy as possible so squares barely touch each other).

Now picture a rectangle with a fixed area. If you increase one side length, the other side length has to shrink to keep the same area. What happens to the perimeter (the sum of all four sides) as you do this?
Direction: as the two side lengths move apart from each other, the perimeter grows. As they move closer together — toward being equal, i.e., a square — the perimeter shrinks. This is the exact same pattern from the Concept Check above, just applied to a sum (perimeter) instead of a product.

title: Venn Diagrams and Overlapping Groups
text:
1 Overview
A Venn diagram is a picture made of overlapping circles that helps organize groups of things and show how they relate. Each circle represents one group, and where two circles overlap represents things that belong to both groups. When a word problem gives you a total, describes two overlapping groups, and tells you how many things are in “both,” the trick is to label the overlap with a variable (like \(x\)) and carefully write an equation using the total: \[\text{total} = (\text{group 1}) + (\text{group 2}) - (\text{both}) + (\text{neither})\]






ImportantCommon Mistake



It’s tempting to just add the two group sizes together, but that always double-counts the overlap. Whenever a problem gives you two overlapping groups, ask yourself: “Did I subtract the ‘both’ group exactly once?”








NoteConcept Check



\(30\) students are surveyed. \(20\) like art, \(15\) like music, and \(8\) like both. If you just added \(20 + 15\), what mistake would you be making, and what should you do instead?






TipShow Answer





Adding \(20 + 15 = 35\) double-counts the \(8\) students who like both — they got counted once in the “art” group and again in the “music” group. To fix it, subtract the overlap once: \(20 + 15 - 8 = 27\) students like at least one.








alt text


A good habit is to fill in a Venn diagram from the inside out: start with the overlap (the “both” region), then work outward to fill in each “only” region, and finally use the leftover space for “neither.” Once every region is labeled, all the numbers should add up to the total.



2 Practice Questions

At Riverside Summer Camp, there are \(80\) campers. The number who like swimming is twice the number who like archery. Six campers like both activities. If \(14\) campers like neither activity, how many campers like archery only, and how many like swimming only? 
In a class of \(30\) students, \(18\) play soccer and \(15\) play basketball. If \(8\) students play both sports, how many students play neither sport?
There are \(96\) creatures at Monster Academy. The number of creatures with claws is three times the number of creatures with wings. One-third of the creatures with wings also have claws. If \(8\) creatures have neither wings nor claws, how many creatures have claws?
In a survey of \(50\) students, \(60\%\) said they like pizza and \(44\%\) said they like tacos. If \(10\) students like neither food, what percent of all students like both pizza and tacos?
At a school, every student takes at least one elective: art or music. \(45\) students take art, \(38\) take music, and \(15\) take both. How many students are there in total?
At Dragon Middle School, \(72\) students are in the coding club, the robotics club, or both. The number in the coding club is twice the number in the robotics club. Nine students are in both clubs. How many students are in the coding club only (not robotics)?
In a group of \(40\) friends, everyone likes hiking, camping, or both — nobody likes neither. \(25\) friends like hiking and \(22\) like camping. How many friends like both activities?
A pet store surveyed \(60\) customers: \(35\) own a dog, \(20\) own a cat, and \(12\) own both. How many customers own neither a dog nor a cat?

title: Mean, Median, Mode, and Range
text:
1 Overview
When we describe a characteristics of numbers with a single value, we usually use one of four tools: mean, median, mode, and range. It’s easy to mix these up, so here’s a clear breakdown.
The mean is what most people call “the average.” You add up every number in the set, then divide by how many numbers there are: \[\text{mean} = \frac{\text{sum of all values}}{\text{number of values}}\]
The median is the middle number once the set is arranged in order from least to greatest. If there’s an even number of values, there isn’t one single middle number, so you average the two middle numbers: \[\text{median} = \frac{a + b}{2} \quad \text{(where } a \text{ and } b \text{ are the two middle values)}\]
The mode is the value (or values) that shows up the most often, a set can have one mode, more than one mode, or no mode at all if every value appears the same number of times.
The range tells you how spread out the data is: \[\text{range} = \text{largest value} - \text{smallest value}\]






NoteConcept Check



A data set is: \(3, 3, 7, 9, 12\). Without doing much calculating, which of the four measures (mean, median, mode, range) can you find fastest just by looking?






TipShow Answer





Mode (\(3\), since it repeats) and range (\(12 - 3 = 9\), just one subtraction) are both fast. This set is already sorted, so the median just needs you to spot the middle value: \(7\). Mean is the only one that truly needs you to add everything up and divide.





A useful way to keep mean and median straight: mean involves math (adding and dividing), while median is about middle (finding the middle spot after sorting). One more idea worth knowing is the weighted average. If you’re combining two groups that are different sizes, you can’t just average their averages, you have to account for how many items are in each group: \[\text{weighted average} = \frac{(\text{sum of group 1}) + (\text{sum of group 2})}{(\text{count of group 1}) + (\text{count of group 2})}\]
For example, if one class of \(20\) students averages \(85\) on a test and another class of \(30\) students averages \(90\), the \(30\)-person class should “count more” toward the overall average because it has more students.






NoteConcept Check



Two classes take the same quiz. Class A (\(10\) students) averages \(80\). Class B (\(20\) students) averages \(90\). Without doing the full calculation, will the combined average be closer to \(80\), closer to \(90\), or exactly in the middle (\(85\))?






TipShow Answer





Closer to \(\mathbf{90}\). Class B has twice as many students, so it “pulls” the weighted average toward its own score more than Class A does. The exact answer is \(\dfrac{10(80)+20(90)}{30} = \dfrac{800+1800}{30} = \dfrac{2600}{30} \approx 86.7\), which is indeed closer to \(90\) than to \(80\).








2 Practice Questions

Six kittens at a shelter weigh \(7, 9, 10, 12, 14,\) and \(8\) pounds. Find the mean weight.
A vet recorded the ages (in months) of 7 puppies: \(3, 5, 2, 8, 4, 6, 5\). Find the median age.
The heights (in inches) of 8 sunflowers were measured: \(40, 55, 48, 60, 52, 45, 58, 50\). Find the median height.
The number of books read by 9 students last month: \(2, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 3, 4, 6\). Find the mode(s).
The high temperature each day this week (in °F): \(72, 68, 75, 70, 74, 69, 71\). Find the range.
The mean score on \(5\) tests is \(84\). The first four scores were \(80, 90, 78,\) and \(88\). What was the score on the fifth test?
A list of five numbers, when arranged from least to greatest, looks like this: \(4, 9, 12, x, 20\). What is the smallest whole number \(x\) could be, if the median of the list must still equal \(12\)?
The chart shows push-up counts for gym class: Andy did \(8\), Bella did \(12\), Carlos did \(10\), Dana did \(8\), Evan did \(14\), and Farah did \(8\). Find the mean, median, mode, and range of the push-up counts. 
Mr. Kim’s math class has \(20\) students with an average test score of \(85\). Ms. Lopez’s math class has \(30\) students with an average test score of \(90\). What is the average test score for all \(50\) students combined?
A set of five whole numbers (each between \(1\) and \(10\)) has a mode of \(7\), and \(7\) appears exactly twice. No other number in the set may repeat. Write one possible set of five numbers that fits these rules. (There is more than one correct answer — just make sure yours follows the rules!)
The mean of \(6\) numbers is \(15\). A \(7\)th number is added to the set, and the new mean becomes \(16\). What is the value of the \(7\)th number?
Over her first \(5\) basketball games, a player scored a mean of \(18\) points per game. In her next \(3\) games, she scored \(20, 25,\) and \(15\) points. What is her new mean, across all \(8\) games?

title: Homepage
text:
After looking over your final, I wrote out a couple review guides. After you go through them (in the order below) you should be able understand the parts of your test that where a little tricky!



Work through the guides in this order. Each one builds a little bit on the habits from the one before it. 1. Mean, Median, Mode, and Range 2. Venn Diagrams and Overlapping Groups 3. Optimization 4. Probability and Counting 5. Circles, Arc Length, and Sector Area

text: After looking over your final, I wrote out a couple review guides. After you go through them (in the order below) you should be able understand the parts of your test that where a little tricky!

title: Homepage
text: Work through the guides in this order. Each one builds a little bit on the habits from the one before it. 1. Mean, Median, Mode, and Range 2. Venn Diagrams and Overlapping Groups 3. Optimization 4. Probability and Counting 5. Circles, Arc Length, and Sector Area

title: Homepage
text:
2.1 Quick Formula Reference







Topic
Formula




Mean
\(\text{mean} = \dfrac{\text{sum of all values}}{\text{number of values}}\)


Median (even count)
\(\text{median} = \dfrac{a + b}{2}\)


Range
\(\text{range} = \text{largest value} - \text{smallest value}\)


Two overlapping groups
\(\text{total} = (\text{group 1}) + (\text{group 2}) - (\text{both}) + (\text{neither})\)


Independent events
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \times P(B)\)


Probability without replacement
\(P(A \text{ then } B) = P(A) \times P(B \mid A \text{ happened})\)


Circle area
\(A = \pi r^2\)


Circle circumference
\(C = 2\pi r\)


Sector area
\(\text{sector area} = \dfrac{\text{angle}}{360} \times \pi r^2\)


Arc length
\(\text{arc length} = \dfrac{\text{angle}}{360} \times 2\pi r\)

title: Probability and Counting
text:
1 Overview
Probability measures how likely something is to happen, and it’s always a fraction: the number of favorable outcomes divided by the total number of possible outcomes. \[P(\text{event}) = \frac{\text{favorable outcomes}}{\text{total outcomes}}\] To use this correctly, you need to count carefully and stay organized: for something like rolling two dice, it helps to write out a table or list of every possible combination rather than trying to count in your head!






NoteConcept Check



You roll two dice and want the probability the sum is \(7\). Before counting anything, how many total possible outcomes are there when two dice are rolled?






TipShow Answer





\(36\) total outcomes — there are \(6\) possibilities for the first die and \(6\) for the second, so \(6 \times 6 = 36\).





Some events are independent, which means the outcome of one event has no effect on the outcome of the next. Flipping a coin twice, rolling dice, or shooting free throws (when told the shooting percentage stays the same) are all independent events. When you want the probability that two independent events both happen, you multiply their individual probabilities together: \[P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \times P(B)\]






ImportantCommon Mistake



If a problem says an event happens with some probability “each time” (like a free-throw percentage), don’t just report that single probability as your final answer when the question asks about two or more events in a row. You need to multiply the probability by itself once for every event.


Other events are dependent, which usually happens when you’re removing items from a group without putting them back — like pulling two candies from a jar one at a time. In this case, the second event’s probability depends on what happened in the first draw, because the total number of items (and possibly the number of favorable items) has changed: \[P(A \text{ then } B) = P(A) \times P(B \mid A \text{ happened})\] You still multiply the two probabilities together, but the second fraction will look different from the first because the group has shrunk.






NoteConcept Check



A bag has marbles. Which situation below is dependent, and which is independent? (a) Drawing a marble, recording its color, and putting it back before drawing again. (b) Drawing a marble and keeping it out, then drawing a second one.






TipShow Answer






is independent — putting the marble back means the bag is exactly the same for the second draw. (b) is dependent — removing the marble changes both the total count and possibly the number of favorable outcomes for the second draw.






Finally, counting problems ask “how many different ways can this happen?” For simple situations, the counting principle says you multiply the number of choices at each step together. For arranging a group of different items in order (like lining people up for a photo), you multiply by one fewer each time, this is called a permutation: for \(n\) items, there are \(n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots \times 1\) ways to arrange them, written as \(n!\) (“\(n\) factorial”).



2 Practice Questions

A bag has \(4\) red marbles, \(5\) blue marbles, and \(3\) green marbles. If one marble is drawn at random, what is the probability it is blue?
Two coins are flipped. List all the possible outcomes, then find the probability that both coins land on heads. 
Two dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum of the two dice is exactly \(8\)? 
Two dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum of the two dice is greater than \(9\)?
Maria makes \(70\%\) of her free throws. What is the probability that she makes her next two free throws in a row?
A spinner lands on red with probability \(\dfrac{1}{3}\). If Jordan spins the spinner twice, what is the probability that it does not land on red either time?
A weighted coin lands on heads \(80\%\) of the time. If it is flipped three times, what is the probability that all three flips are heads?
A bag contains \(3\) black balls and \(6\) white balls. If two balls are drawn from the bag one at a time, without putting the first one back, what is the probability that both balls are white?
A jar has \(5\) lemon candies and \(3\) cherry candies. Two candies are picked at random, one after another, without replacement. What is the probability that both candies are lemon?
Using the same jar from Question 9 (\(5\) lemon, \(3\) cherry), what is the probability that the first candy picked is lemon and the second candy picked is cherry?
How many different ways can \(4\) students (Ann, Ben, Cara, and Dan) be lined up in a row for a photo?
A sandwich shop lets you choose \(1\) type of bread (\(3\) choices), \(1\) type of filling (\(3\) choices), and \(1\) type of cheese (\(2\) choices). How many different sandwiches are possible?
